annotations.label: Combination

anchor.snippet: a combination is a selection of items from a set that has distinct members

annotations.note: A combination (selection of distinct members) is exactly a finite subset, formalized as `Finset`, though no declaration is named "combination".

annotations.label: k-combination

anchor.snippet: a k -combination of a set S is a subset of k distinct elements of S

annotations.note: `Finset.powersetCard n s` is the finset of all k-element subsets, and `mem_powersetCard` characterizes a k-combination as `s ⊆ t ∧ card s = n`.

annotations.label: Equality of combinations

anchor.snippet: two combinations are identical if and only if each combination has the same members

annotations.note: `Finset.ext`/`Finset.ext_iff` states two finsets are equal iff they have the same members.

annotations.label: Number of k-combinations is binomial coefficient

anchor.snippet: is equal to the binomial coefficient

annotations.note: `Finset.card_powersetCard` proves `card (powersetCard n s) = Nat.choose (card s) n`, exactly the count of k-combinations.

annotations.label: Factorial formula for k-combinations

anchor.snippet: which using factorial notation can be compactly expressed as

annotations.note: `Nat.choose_eq_factorial_div_factorial` gives `choose n k = n! / (k! * (n-k)!)` for `k ≤ n`.

annotations.label: Combination without repetition

anchor.snippet: A combination is a selection of n things taken k at a time without repetition

annotations.note: Selection without repetition is a k-element subset, captured by `Finset.powersetCard`.

annotations.label: Combination with repetition

anchor.snippet: the terms k -combination with repetition

annotations.note: A combination with repetition (multiset of fixed size) is formalized as the type `Sym α k`.

annotations.label: Poker hand as 5-combination

anchor.snippet: a poker hand can be described as a 5-combination

annotations.note: This is an illustrative example with no corresponding Mathlib declaration.

annotations.label: Binomial coefficient via defining relation

anchor.snippet: for all natural numbers k at once by the relation

annotations.note: The generating relation `(x+y)^n = ∑ choose n k * ...` is the binomial theorem `add_pow`, though Mathlib defines `choose` inductively rather than by this relation.

annotations.label: Coefficients count k-combinations

anchor.snippet: To see that these coefficients count k -combinations from S

annotations.note: That choose counts k-subsets is `Finset.card_powersetCard`, but the specific generating-function-coefficient bridge is not a single named declaration.

annotations.label: Pascal's recursion relation

anchor.snippet: one can use (in addition to the basic cases already given) the recursion relation

annotations.note: `Nat.choose_succ_succ` gives `choose (n+1) (k+1) = choose n k + choose n (k+1)`, Pascal's recursion (also `Nat.choose_eq_choose_pred_add`).

annotations.label: Formula for individual binomial coefficient

anchor.snippet: it is more practical to use the formula

annotations.note: `Nat.choose_eq_descFactorial_div_factorial` gives `choose n k = descFactorial n k / k!`, the falling-product-over-k! formula.

annotations.label: Symmetry of binomial coefficients

anchor.snippet: canceling them out gives the relation

annotations.note: `Nat.choose_symm` proves `choose n (n-k) = choose n k` for `k ≤ n`.

annotations.label: Symmetric factorial formula

anchor.snippet: Finally there is a formula which exhibits this symmetry directly

annotations.note: `Nat.choose_mul_factorial_mul_factorial` gives the symmetric identity `choose n k * k! * (n-k)! = n!`.

annotations.label: Adjacent relations in Pascal's triangle

anchor.snippet: follow relations between adjacent numbers in Pascal's triangle in all three directions

annotations.note: Adjacency relations are formalized, e.g. `Nat.succ_mul_choose_eq` and `Nat.choose_succ_right_eq`.

annotations.label: Counting five-card hands

anchor.snippet: one can compute the number of five-card hands possible from a standard fifty-two card deck

annotations.note: The specific numeric computation `choose 52 5 = 2598960` is not present as a declaration.

annotations.label: Combinatorial number system bijection

anchor.snippet: which establishes a bijection from an interval

annotations.note: Mathlib has colex ordering of finsets (`Finset.Colex`, `orderIsoColex` for all subsets) but not the explicit combinadic bijection from `[0, choose n k)` to k-combinations.

annotations.label: Enumeration algorithm for k-combinations

anchor.snippet: One way is to track k index numbers of the elements selected

annotations.note: This describes an enumeration algorithm with no corresponding formalized procedure.

annotations.label: k-combination with repetition (multisubset)

anchor.snippet: A k - combination with repetitions , or k - multicombination , or multisubset of size k from a set S

annotations.note: A multisubset of size k from S is exactly `Sym α k`, the type of multisets of cardinality k over α.

annotations.label: Multisubsets count Diophantine solutions

anchor.snippet: The number of multisubsets of size k is then the number of nonnegative integer

annotations.note: The multisubset count is formalized via `Sym.card_sym_eq_multichoose`, but the explicit equality with the number of nonnegative integer solutions of `x₁+...+xₙ=k` is not a named declaration.

annotations.label: n multichoose k notation

anchor.snippet: the number of such k -multisubsets is denoted by

annotations.note: `Nat.multichoose n k` is defined as the number of size-k multisets from an n-element type.

annotations.label: Multichoose in terms of binomial coefficients

anchor.snippet: can also be given in terms of binomial coefficients

annotations.note: `Nat.multichoose_eq` proves `multichoose n k = (n + k - 1).choose k`.

annotations.label: Stars and bars proof

anchor.snippet: This relationship can be easily proved using a representation known as stars and bars

annotations.note: `Sym.card_sym_eq_choose` proves `card (Sym α k) = choose (card α + k - 1) k`, the stars-and-bars result, though the stars-and-bars argument itself is not a separate object.

annotations.label: Multichoose identity

anchor.snippet: there are several relationships between these multichoose expressions

annotations.note: Various multichoose lemmas exist (`multichoose_eq`, `multichoose_succ_succ`, etc.) but the specific cited multichoose identities are not individually named.

annotations.label: Counting donut multisubsets

anchor.snippet: if you have four types of donuts

annotations.note: This is a worked numeric example with no corresponding declaration.

annotations.label: Total number of subsets is 2^n

anchor.section: Number of k -combinations for all k
anchor.snippet: The number of k -combinations for all k is the number of subsets of a set of n elements

annotations.note: `Finset.card_powerset` gives `card (powerset s) = 2 ^ card s`, and `Nat.sum_range_choose` proves `∑ choose n k = 2^n`.

annotations.label: Eight subsets of three cards

anchor.section: Number of k -combinations for all k
anchor.snippet: Given 3 cards numbered 1 to 3, there are 8 distinct combinations

annotations.note: This is a concrete enumeration example with no corresponding declaration.

annotations.label: Sampling a random combination

anchor.snippet: One way to select a k -combination efficiently from a population of size n

annotations.note: This describes a sampling algorithm with no corresponding formalized procedure.

annotations.label: Objects into bins is multinomial coefficient

anchor.section: Number of ways to put objects into bins
anchor.snippet: The number of ways to put objects into bins is given by the multinomial coefficient

annotations.note: `Nat.multinomial` defines the coefficient (documented as counting arrangements), but no theorem proves the bijective "ways to put objects into bins" count.

annotations.label: Binomial coefficient as two-bin special case

anchor.section: Number of ways to put objects into bins
anchor.snippet: The binomial coefficient is the special case where k items go into the chosen bin

annotations.note: `Nat.binomial_eq_choose` proves `multinomial {a,b} f = (f a + f b).choose (f a)`, the two-bin special case.
